sections.heading: The English Alphabet
sections.body:
English has 26 letters, divided into two groups:

Vowels (5): A, E, I, O, U — these are the core sounds of English. Every word needs at least one vowel.

Consonants (21): B, C, D, F, G, H, J, K, L, M, N, P, Q, R, S, T, V, W, X, Y, Z

sections.heading: Short Vowel Sounds
sections.body:
Short vowels are quick, clipped sounds:

• A as in "cat", "bat", "hat"
• E as in "bed", "red", "pen"
• I as in "sit", "hit", "bit"
• O as in "hot", "pot", "dot"
• U as in "cup", "bus", "run"

sections.heading: Long Vowel Sounds
sections.body:
Long vowels say their own name:

• A as in "cake", "make", "lake"
• E as in "these", "tree", "free"
• I as in "bike", "kite", "time"
• O as in "home", "bone", "tone"
• U as in "cute", "huge", "use"

sections.heading: Basic Greetings
sections.body:
Hello! / Hi! — Informal greeting
Good morning! — Before noon
Good afternoon! — 12pm to 5pm
Good evening! — After 5pm
How are you? — Asking about well-being
I'm fine, thank you. — Standard reply

jargon.definition: A letter (A, E, I, O, U) that represents an open sound. Every English word has at least one vowel.
A letter that is not a vowel. Consonants are made by blocking or restricting airflow.
A unit of pronunciation with one vowel sound. "Hello" = hel-lo (2 syllables). "Cat" = 1 syllable.
The way a word sounds when spoken. Same spelling can have different pronunciations.

summary: Master the 26 letters and their sounds before moving on. Practice saying each vowel pair (short and long) out loud for 5 minutes daily.
